speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 24th of March, 1995. We are in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so if you'd like to introduce your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my oldest son, Herman; Sari, my daughter; and Louis, another son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you've got three other children as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They can't be here. They have business.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the young daughter lives up north--

speaker: interviewer
text: So is there anything you'd like to say about your mother and the kind of person she is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be funny. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she's been very supportive over the years. It hasn't been easy. She's tried her best under the circumstances. We have talked a lot about the past. We do ask, and she's always ready to explain and tell us of all her experiences. Admittedly, sometimes it does get a bit depressing, because we hear it a lot. Particularly, as the years are getting on, we want to make sure we know everything. And it's very repetitive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But mum-- mum never spoke about it as much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dad was the one that was always--

speaker: interviewee
text: --talking about it. And he was the more emotional one.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went through a lot more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and mum was always able to talk about it in a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in a more-- in a clearer manner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She doesn't seem to have-- I mean, to us, anyway, she doesn't seem to have been as traumatized as dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he went through a lot more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's it like for you hearing these experiences, though? Is it difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] We've lived with it all our lives, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: We always used to look at the people next door as our grandparents, because other children had grandparents, and we didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not so much a stigma. It's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, we-- we felt that we-- there was something different about us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in terms of being Jewish? Was that an issue for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, being one of only two families in Loftus that were Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's right. And I-- I was very-- I felt very different-- not so much about being Jewish, but coming from Europe. We just felt different, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way we-- I did. I did. I remember I used to-- my mother used to let me go-- just to make me go to school with different clothes, and-- and other children wondered why I would-- had to wear a hat when it was cold, [BACKROUND NOISE] and why I had a different jacket, and that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, you should have told me before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know. [LAUGHTER] I know. That's-- well, I guess things are coming out.

speaker: interviewee
text: First time I hear of it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I thought mum really assimilated-- our both-- our parents assimilated very, very quickly--

speaker: interviewee
text: --living out at Loftus. You know, mum was very involved in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, the school. And mum was the treasurer, you know, for a long time. And-- and she was a bit of an identity at-- at Loftus. And then when I went to high school at Heathcote, I was only one of two of the Jewish kids there. There was myself and my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked in the canteen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but mum was always around, you know? But I was lucky, because it was a very small and new school. And-- and I never had to hide the fact that I was Jewish or anything, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: None of them-- well, we all went--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were just the Melkmans. We were very famous. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always getting into trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were different. [LAUGHTER] We were different because we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps, if we had lived in the eastern suburbs, we wouldn't have stood out as much.

speaker: interviewee
text: But because we were a migrant, Jewish family living in Loftus, we did-- we were different, you know, in lots of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the thing is, is that none of the other-- I know, at school, that none of the other kids had any relationships, or had known any other Jewish people. So I mean, it's not what they did know. It was more what they didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only-- I only encountered anti-Semitism, you know, once I went to uni, or once I began my business life. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --it kind of was a bit of a shock--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a couple of minor instances, but I wasn't put down.

speaker: interviewee
text: You were in Betar when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I wouldn't take any-- wouldn't take any shit from anyone, basically.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I [BACKROUND NOISE] went overseas on my own when I was in my early 20s, because I wanted to reunite with some of the people whom my parents had spoken about. And that was a very traumatic experience for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 'cause I was telling them that I was living in England for a year. [BACKROUND NOISE] And this Audrey and Vivian, that I'm still friendly with them-- the girls-- and she went to see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm still very friendly with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I suppose they're our extended family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because one of the girls always said, that's my big sister out of Australia. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that what was traumatic for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just hearing about the experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and hearing about a lot of things about my parents-- that they hadn't told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just how they-- how brave they were, how they came back with nothing, and they still picked up all the threads, and got themselves together and-- and left for a new country; [BACKROUND NOISE] and then going to some of the memorial places, like Anne Frank's house and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think the thing that-- that really got me, was when I visited these people in England-- was that they were able to show me a letter--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that-- that-- that the father, like Mr. [? Fine, ?] who was a friend of mum's father, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones who gave me the wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, it said, look, we-- we know what's happening. We can see what's happening. Come over. Leave. Leave everything. Come to England. We'll look after you, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was in that letter too.

speaker: interviewee
text: That letter came back to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and I could see it. I suppose that's the-- the annoying thing, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in the meantime, it was-- Holland was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --occupied. And they got the letter-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: They, being in England, could see the things--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that-- that people who were being affected the most couldn't see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when he got that letter--

speaker: interviewee
text: --back, he said, if anyone ever comes back after the war, I'll do for them what I can-- anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose that's what got me. I thought--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mind you, in the beginning of the war, my father sent them a letter. There's going to be a terrible war. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: --better take your family and come here, to Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: See how naive the-- I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was neutral in the previous war. So they thought, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, people can't understand how all these Jews were killed. But I guess, in a funny sort of a way--

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was a sheep syndrome, wasn't it? You know, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --they didn't realize what was happening until it was too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah, but, you see, there wasn't any television. There was no--

speaker: interviewee
text: --no, you know? And-- and that was, I think, probably, one of the big reasons.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it wouldn't happen today. I mean, I hope it wouldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it couldn't, with all the communications.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we might leave it there. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Blee, ba da, ba da-- that's all folks. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who are the people in these photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're my parents-- father, David Blokjesman; my mother, Sara Blokjesman, maiden name, Bajaviei.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when were they taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father could have been anytime-- before 1940, anyhow. And my mother, maybe 1941. I don't know, if that's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they taken in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandmother-- mother's mother. Her name is Esperance Bajaviei. And that was taken-- oh, I don't know-- before 1940, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's the grandmother that lived with you in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are my two brothers. The first one is Jack Blokjesman. The other is Meyer Blokjesman. And-- the first one was taken, I would think, before 1940; the second one, maybe after 1940. Yeah, that was the later one-- latest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first one is the one on the left. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Jack-- here, the dark one-- the dark photo. And Jack died in Bergen-Belsen. And Meyer went with the first call-ups when he was 16 year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are two very good friends of mine. They are, to me, the righteous people. They're not Jewish. She-- Bertha-- used to work for my mother, and became a good friend to me. Her husband, Hank, used to help my father, often in the busy season. And they received illegal letters from my husband. And Ida gave them to me, and later kept them. That's Bertha and Hank from Breifort. That's the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you keep in touch with them much after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes. I went to visit her. She only died last year, or two years ago; and he, a few years before.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are two photos of myself. The first one was taken soon after I met my husband in 19-- 4-- 40-- 40, 41-- 40-- 42?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the one on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the light-- the light one. And the other one was taken after my parents went, and after my fiance went. You can see the difference in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the expressions on your faces, aren't they? The eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother-- father's mother. Her name is Charlotte-- sorry-- Charlotte Blokjesman, [LAUGHS] after whom I'm named. She was 90-years-old when the Germans took her away on a hands-cart, because she couldn't walk. She hardly came out of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ready. This is my husband, Nick Melkman, taken in, I would say, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is [INAUDIBLE]? Where was that, sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of my wedding photos. As you'll notice, I'm not dressed as a bride, as it was straight after the war, and there were no relatives, no parents-- nobody present. Besides that, my husband didn't have any clothes. He had to go in his uniform. So we were quite happy to go like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 6th of February, 1946, in England, Blackpool.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a conscious decision not to wear white?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I could have had it, because, you know, they said, if you want white, you can have it. You can have anything you want. And I said, I had a suit made for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide not to wear white?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were no relatives present. My parents were not there. His parents were not there-- didn't know anyone. We were all strangers. The main thing is, you know, I'll still get married. It doesn't matter how. And in Holland, it quite often happened that they didn't go in white. They'd go in-- in a suit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish wedding in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I've got a photo of the Jewish bit. Did-- did you want it?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So are the people that you stayed with in this picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, when you were having the wedding, it was a happy time, and yet, a sad time as well--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very happy to get married, of course, but very sad, because no-- nobody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were thinking about that at the time?

speaker: interviewer
text: You look quite wistful in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that is a photo of the house built after it arrived here in prefabricated form. We had to buy the block of land, put the foundations down-- and away we go. The house was built.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did your husband actually build it himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was another Dutchman who built his own house like that. And he had a job at the water port. And he built a few houses, gave up his job, and became one of the big-- biggest builders-- [? Karl ?] [? Kampenhout. ?] You've heard of [? Karl ?] [? Kampenhout-- ?] big builders-- made unnecessary mistakes on our house, you know, but he learned. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the guinea pigs? Oh. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the latest photo of Nick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just before he died. In 1980-- he died first of January. In 1984, it would have been taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself with all the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you point out who-- each of them are?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was taken on Sari's 40th birthday, which would have been 1949 and-- 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you point out each of your children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Ha. Where's my glasses? [LAUGHS] Oh. I've got it there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or just name them from-- from the left, if you like.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Just start from the left and go over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. The first? The-- the little one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that's Benji. That's a-- wait a minute. [LAUGHS] 1, 2, 3, 4-- can I have a look? Can I take it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, hang-- hang on a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lester, Jacqueline, Sari, in front there, Louis, myself, Herman, and David.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the yellow star which we had to wear. This is actually the one Nick, my husband, wore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to wear that as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to wear it, too, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was sewn on the clothes. You had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get it from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Jewish authorities. We had to buy it. I think they were $0.20. I'm not sure. I think it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it that you had to start wearing them?

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Holland, was there any support from the-- the non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, but it didn't last long, because you know, the Germans oppressed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they actually wore the stars as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- some of them did, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --quite good that they actually were supportive of you. Mm-hmm. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a letter which I got to tell me that my work, my job, was ended-- because being Jewish-- after I've been working eight years with that firm. This-- the letter is from the [NON-ENGLISH], which means-- who took over from the-- from the Jewish authorities. How would you call it? An-- [INAUDIBLE]? I don't know how you would call it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The over-- the-- the new boss-- the new boss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a Jewish firm, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It used to be a Jewish firm, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- basically, the form just says that you're being dismissed.

speaker: interviewee
text: No Jewish working there anymore. You're dismissed, yeah-- with a month's salary.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in-- this is illegal letter sent from the camp. He must have given it to somebody who had authority to get out of the camp. How he did it, I don't know. But it was illegal-- sent to a friend, the people I showed you before, on the-- Bertha and Hank. And you can see it is censored. But it still came through. It's one of the many illegal letters he sent--

speaker: interviewee
text: --either from prison, or-- or this is from the camp, Flossenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these letters, he would write about his situation, but it was in code.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some that were passed on to you, and some you never saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this one proves that I've been in the train-- my name, and that I have been traveling in the train, and where I was liberated-- the different things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- it speaks for itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: The document speaks for itself. It's an official document.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is about a lost transport. It's the train what was liberated in Trobitz. And I have an a reunion this year-- a 50 years liberation of Bergen-Belsen. And near Trobitz, where we were liberated, they're putting up an monument for the people who died in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can't attend it, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't attend it. I'm too far away, yeah-- in Australia. If I would be in Israel or Holland, I would have done it.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would have had somebody who-- family died in the train, I would have done the effort. But no relations of me died in the train, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you keep in touch with anybody who was on the train?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape five, of the interview with Charlotte Melkman. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the 24th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's the end of the whole interview.
